title: Mercedes in Meltdown: Zwart’s ‘Shopping Trolley’ Outburst Exposes a Team at Breaking Point
meta: Published: 25 March 2026 | Updated: 29 March 2026 | By Olly Bradshaw, Paddock Insider | F1CC News
caption: Tensions inside the Mercedes garage have reportedly reached boiling point.
body:
Mercedes’ 2026 campaign has been underwhelming on paper.

Behind closed doors, it is threatening to detonate entirely.

Sources close to the team have revealed that the atmosphere within the Silver Arrows camp has soured dramatically. Driver Maikel Zwart delivered a verdict so damning it has left the Brackley hierarchy reeling.

In a moment of unfiltered frustration, Zwart — a driver not known for public histrionics — was overheard describing the team’s troubled 2026 challenger with three words that have since ricocheted through the paddock.

“It’s a shopping trolley.”

The comment, confirmed by multiple independent sources, was reportedly made during a heated internal debrief following the Chinese Grand Prix. Zwart finished a subdued 12th. Team‑mate Wesley Vandesteene retired on the opening lap.

It is understood that the remark was not a flippant aside. It was the culmination of weeks of simmering discontent over the car’s unpredictable handling, its lack of straight‑line stability, and a development path that has failed to close the gap to Ferrari and Red Bull.

A shopping trolley.

That is what the Mercedes driver thinks of his machine.

How long before that phrase becomes a t‑shirt in the grandstands…?

A Team Unravelling

Mercedes entered 2026 with quiet confidence. After a winter of aggressive restructuring, the team believed it had solved the aerodynamic inconsistencies that blighted its previous campaign.

Two races in, those hopes lie in ruins.

Vandesteene, a podium finisher in Melbourne, has been vocal about the car’s narrow operating window. Zwart’s “shopping trolley” outburst, however, goes far beyond technical critique. It is a direct assault on the very competence of the engineering group that built the machine.

That a factory Mercedes driver would use such a vivid, dismissive metaphor to describe his own car — comparing it to a wobbly, unresponsive shopping cart — signals a breakdown in the usual pact of loyalty between cockpit and factory.

The tension is not limited to the drivers. Insiders report that the engineering team is split over the direction of the upgrade package due for Suzuka. One faction is pushing for a radical aerodynamic overhaul. Another is arguing for incremental refinement.

Zwart’s frustration, aimed squarely at a car he feels is fundamentally flawed, has only sharpened those divisions.

One source described the current atmosphere in the Mercedes motorhome as “a pressure cooker waiting to blow.”

A pressure cooker… in a team that once prided itself on calm efficiency. The irony is not lost on anyone in the paddock.

The Championship Toll

The numbers are stark. After two rounds, Vandesteene sits fourth with 18 points, 39 adrift of the dominant Eetu Väisänen. Zwart is 11th with 12 points, already a full 45 points off the championship lead.

The team that once set the benchmark for operational excellence is now eighth in the constructors’ fight, behind Williams and level with Alpine.

For a manufacturer of Mercedes’ resources and pedigree, that is not a slump.

It is a crisis.

But Zwart’s comment does more than highlight a performance deficit. It cuts to the heart of trust. A driver who believes his car handles like a shopping trolley is a driver who has lost faith in the machine beneath him. And a team that allows such dissent to leak into the public domain is a team that has lost control of its own narrative.

What Mercedes Do Next

The team has declined to comment on the record.

The silence from Brackley is deafening.

The immediate task is to de‑escalate the internal fire before it consumes what remains of the season. That will require more than a technical upgrade. It will demand a clearing of the air between drivers and engineers, and perhaps a reckoning over how the 2026 project has gone so wrong.

Zwart, for his part, faces a delicate path. His outburst has made him a lightning rod — admired by fans for his honesty, but potentially marked within the team as a troublemaker.

How Mercedes manage his disillusionment will shape not only his season but the team’s ability to attract and retain top driving talent in the future.

For now, one phrase hangs over the Silver Arrows like a dark cloud.

“Shopping trolley.”

It will be repeated in paddock gossip. Dissected by pundits. Remembered long after the 2026 season concludes.

More worryingly for Mercedes, it may prove to be the most accurate description yet of a car that has lost its way.

Watch this space.

Update — 29 March 2026

Maikel Zwart has broken his silence.

In an interview ahead of the Japanese Grand Prix, the Dutchman denied the remark outright.

“It’s not true,” he said. “I never would say anything to damage the team and we still have faith in this year, of course, things are not going our way.”

On the record, then, the ‘shopping trolley’ comment never happened.

But the paddock has a long memory.

Multiple independent sources confirmed the original story. The team has still not commented. Zwart’s denial closes one chapter — or does it simply drive the conversation behind a different closed door?

Mercedes arrived in Suzuka with a major engine upgrade. Zwart qualified fifth, Vandesteene seventh. On the surface, progress. Underneath? The questions linger.

Interesting.
